title: Quick check: coordinating with the SOC on SIEM

questions.prompt: The lesson names four properties of a clean SOC reply. Which list captures them?
choices: Factually anchored, scoped to a specific question.

choices.label: Long, detailed, exhaustive, and cc'd to the customer's IT manager.
choices.rationale: Inverted on every property.

choices.label: Casual, friendly, frequent, and one-question-per-day.
choices.rationale: Tone and frequency are not the lesson's properties.

choices.label: Anonymous, terse, demanding, and timestamped.
choices.rationale: Inverted on tone and helpfulness.

questions.explanation: Customer-context-bringing rather than analyst-second-guessing, respectful of what the SOC already weighed. Factually anchored, scoped, customer-context-bringing, and respectful of the SOC's prior work. Those four properties make replies productive.

questions.prompt: Which is a legitimate reason to reply to the SOC on a SIEM Incident Report?
choices: You have learned something during triage the SOC did not have.

choices.label: Your gut feels the SOC got the classification wrong.
choices.rationale: Gut-feel without new signal is second-guessing.

choices.label: You want the SOC to tune the detection so it stops firing.
choices.rationale: Tuning requests go through the exclusion-request flow, not a per-incident reply.

choices.label: You want to show the SOC you read the Evidence carefully.
choices.rationale: The reply is not a status report on your reading.

questions.explanation: Customer-confirmed automation, a recent integration change, an entity's role in a documented workflow. New customer context that the SOC did not have when writing the Recommendation is the primary reply trigger.

questions.prompt: Which reply pattern should you avoid?
choices: 'I think the SOC got this wrong' without new signal: re-litigating the classification.

choices.label: 'Customer's IT manager confirms this service account is part of nightly automation; should that change the classification?'
choices.rationale: That is a good reply: factually anchored, customer-context-bringing.

choices.label: 'Block at firewall is in place. EDR check returned clean. Closing as remediation-complete.'
choices.rationale: That is a good reply: closes the loop factually.

choices.label: Any factual cross-surface finding.
choices.rationale: Cross-surface findings are legitimate reply content.

questions.explanation: Re-litigating the classification without new information is the second-guessing failure mode expressed as a reply.

questions.prompt: The lesson describes the SOC analyst as which kind of counterpart?

choices.label: A vendor-support queue you raise tickets against.
choices.rationale: The SOC is not a support queue.

choices: A colleague doing the same work on a different surface.

choices.label: A higher authority whose disposition you defer to without question.
choices.rationale: You can push back via factual clarifying questions. The line is unilateral override, not disagreement.

choices.label: A junior whose judgement you can correct.
choices.rationale: The SOC has analytic context the tech does not.

questions.explanation: Tone is colleague-to-colleague: specific, respectful, factually anchored, brief. The SOC analyst is a colleague. You bring customer context they lack; they bring analytic context you lack. The tone is peer-to-peer.

questions.prompt: "Asking multiple questions in one reply" is a named common mistake. What is the right alternative?

choices.label: Two to three questions per reply if they are related.
choices.rationale: The lesson is one-per-reply regardless of relatedness.

choices.label: Ask all questions up front so the SOC sees the full picture.
choices.rationale: Forces the SOC to address each in turn and slows everything.

choices.label: Ask no questions; just state findings.
choices.rationale: Overcorrection. Questions are legitimate when scoped.

questions.explanation: Multiple questions slow the SOC's response and lose the thread. One question per reply keeps the exchange focused. If you have two questions, the second reply follows the first answer.

questions.prompt: "Is this a real threat or a false positive?" is the wrong framing. What is a better framing?

choices.label: 'Should I close this incident?' Let the SOC decide.
choices.rationale: Wrong delegation. The disposition is in the Recommendation.

choices: 'Is this finding part of the same incident.

choices.label: 'What is your confidence level on this classification?'
choices.rationale: Confidence-probing is litigating from a different angle.

choices.label: 'Can you explain your reasoning?'
choices.rationale: Open-ended and not scoped. The reasoning is in the annotation.

questions.explanation: Or a separate event?' Specific and scoped to a fact the SOC can answer. Specific, factual questions the SOC can answer without re-classifying are the right shape. Real-threat-vs-false-positive forces re-classification.

questions.prompt: You blocked the firewall destination and EDR returned clean. The Recommendation did not specify what to do if EDR is clean. Which reply is right?

choices.label: 'The Recommendation does not tell me what to do next. Can you clarify?'
choices.rationale: Vague and not factually anchored.

choices.label: 'I disagree that EDR needs to check. Can we close?'
choices.rationale: Second-guessing the dual-action structure after completing both actions.

questions.explanation: EDR check returned no related incidents. Closing as remediation-complete; let me know if further action is needed on the EDR side.' Factually anchored, names both actions and their results, states the plan (close), and offers the SOC a redirect opportunity.

questions.prompt: Two days after a SIEM incident, a similar one on the same customer lands. You want to flag the recurring pattern. Where does the observation go?

choices.label: Reply on the new Incident Report: 'Is this a recurring pattern worth investigating?'
choices.rationale: Wrong reply target. The new incident's Recommendation is what you act on.

choices: Run the new incident through the standard flow.

choices.label: Reply on both Incident Reports asking the analyst to tune the detection.
choices.rationale: Tuning is not a per-incident reply target. The exclusion-request flow is the right channel.

questions.explanation: Surface the pattern observation in your end-of-shift handover to senior. Cross-incident patterns belong at the portfolio level. Surface them to the senior, not as a per-incident SOC reply.

questions.prompt: You have replied to the SOC three times and the thread is going back and forth without converging. What is the right escalation?

choices.label: Reply a fourth time, more emphatically.
choices.rationale: Litigation by repetition.

choices: Surface to senior with the specific exchange.

choices.label: Close the incident without further reply.
choices.rationale: Unilateral closure without resolution.

choices.label: Call Huntress support and ask them to intervene.
choices.rationale: Internal MSP escalation is the right path, not vendor support.

questions.explanation: The senior reviews and either takes over or names the next step. Looped threads are a senior trigger. The senior has the relationship channels and the authority to break the loop.

questions.prompt: A peer writes: "This detection has fired benign three times this month. Can you stop it from firing?" as a reply to the SOC analyst. What is wrong with this reply?

choices.label: Nothing. The SOC can directly tune the detection from a reply.
choices.rationale: Wrong channel. The exclusion-request flow exists so detection changes go through review.

choices: It is a tuning request via the wrong channel.

choices.label: The peer should add more detail about the three incidents.
choices.rationale: More detail in the wrong channel is still the wrong channel.

choices.label: The peer should cc the customer's IT manager.
choices.rationale: Wrong recipient and wrong channel.

questions.explanation: The exclusion-request flow is the right path, not a per-incident SOC reply. Stop-the-detection requests belong to the exclusion-request flow, not to per-incident SOC replies. The formal flow weighs scope and the SOC makes the detection change.
